From the Owen W. Bombard interviews series, 1951-1961, accession 64.167.65
Interview number: 64.167.65.[xxx]
Dictation conducted: 1952-1954
Transcript digitized by staff of Benson Ford Research Center: 2023
OCR: Please note that this file has been made searchable through the use of optical character recognition.
However, the quality of the original materials is such that full text searching is only moderately reliable.

Note to reader:
The memoirs of Charles E. Sorensen were dictated by Sorensen over a period of two years
from October 1952 to October 1954, when the final revised transcript was completed and
sent to Henry Ford II. Sorensen recorded Dictaphone belts at his residences in Detroit and
Miami and sent them to the Ford Archives. These tapes were transcribed and any notes
from Sorensen were added along with facts and figures he requested the archives to
research. The transcripts were then sent back to Sorensen to review and revise at his home
or his occasional trips to the Ford Archives. Almost 200 belts were transcribed and
reviewed over this period, and the following are the finished transcripts.
The intended order of the chapters is unknown, as it was not noted fully anywhere in the
correspondence between Sorensen and the archives. The chapters are arranged
alphabetically in Accession 65 Owen Bombard Interviews Series, the following
arrangement is based on notes in the correspondence between Sorensen and Bombard.

Charles E. Sorensen Contents
1. 	Preface and Index
2. 	Early Years and Henry Ford & The Formative Years
3. 	The Making of Men
4. 	Selden Patents
5. 	Village Industries
6. 	Rouge Expansion
7. 	D.T. & I. Railroad
8. 	European Branches
9. 	The Banking Crisis
10. Philosophical Comments on Manufacturing
11. Engineering Design and Philosophy
12. Mrs. Henry Ford
13. Organization & Administration
14. Henry Ford
15. Amtorg
16. Tractors
17. Diary Notes; 1940-1944

I was born in Copenhagen, Denmark on September 7, 	1881.
My place of birth was at No. 	10 Skittegade, 	Oesterbro, 	Copenhagen,
Denmark. 	My father and mother were both natives of Denmark. 	My
father was born in the parish of Eilstrup, Maarum, 	on the 21st
day of February, 1859. 	His full name was Soren Sorensen.
My mother was born in Copenhagen (Trinitatis), 	on the
first day of November, 	1857. 	Her full name was Eva Christine
Abrahamsen.
I had an ancestoral table drawn by a firm in Copenhagen
and I can tell you the history on my father's 	side on the male
side back to 1685, and on his mother's side back to 1771.
On my father's side in 1685, Marcus Hoeg was a district
judge in the parish of Hoptrup. 	He was buried in Hoptrup in
1729. 	From there down to the father of my father, 	they all
seemed to follow this same judicial work. 	Three of my ancestors
following from here were all parish clerks in their various parishes.
My grandfather on my father's 	side was a pupil of a
seminary, and later teacher at the school in Blidstrup near
Helsinge. 	I cannot remember ever seeing my grandfather who died
in 1887.
My father's mother's name was Ane Margrethe Sorensdatter.
Following through on this maternal side, we have records back to
1771. 	All these ancestors from there on up to my grandmother's

My mother became an orphan at a very early age. 	She
was adopted by an uncle who took her to Sweden. 	This step-
father had a brother and they were engineers for all types of
construction work. 	They built the railroad that ran between
Oslo and Stockholm. 	My mother told me later in years about how
they lived as this railroad was being built. 	They moved up
into different towns along the railroad. 	She described this so
wonderfully to me that when I made my first trip from Oslo to
Stockholm, 	I could really see the things she described to me
in these interesting little spots along the way.
After the construction of this railroad, 	they moved
back to Copenhagen and of course, 	it was there that she met my
father.
My father lived on a farm as a boy. 	He lived close
by the King's farm and summer residence. 	As a boy, he met and
knew King Christian I. 	My father, 	as a young man, went to
Copenhagen as an apprentice in a plant, 	to learn woodworking.
He took drafting lessons and became a Modale Snieger, which means
a model maker. 	He made the models of interiors, 	like stairways
and furnishings and household equipment. 	He had as friends,
several good atheletes and helped them in their training. 	One,
a great runner at one mile distance, 	was named Sorensen. 	My
father called him "little Sorensen." 	"Little Sorensen" went to

The stoves in that day were all made of cast iron;

.
development job on what was then called base burners. 	They

stoves. 	They were laid out in full scale and views of them
were made from all directions. 	The drawings were laid out in
different colors, 	so you would understand which -was the various
views and in making the patterns from these drawings, 	the
pattern makers used trarrnnels and measuring devices for taking
it off of the drawing and then going back and making the
pattern to that scale. 	Some of the parts had quite a lot of
carving on them. 	Of course, 	the carving had to be of such
design that they could be put into the sand and there would be
no back draft on them. 	Much of this carving work was done by
making wax models of them and then taking a plaster cast off
the models.
In this first cast, 	they would lay in a thickness of
wax which was equivalent to what the casting was to be, usually
about 1/8 inch, 	and in some cases, even lighter than that.
The matrix would then be cast, 	the mold opened up,
and the inlay wax removed. 	Then a proper hard red colored wax

In this plant there were machine shops, primarily to
keep the machines that were in the production lines in order.
I began to spend some time in this end of the plant. 	I also
was very keen on the foundry end of it. 	After I was there about

which was always a ten-hour day would be in the pattern shop.
Of course, we had an allowance out for lunch at that period
which was one-half-hour period.
The owner of this business was a. man named Sherman S.
Jewett. 	In my day heWis already ninety years old. 	He died
about 1899 and innnediately there was confusion in the business.
He had two sons. 	Neither one of them seemed to be interested

then began casting around looking for another job and finally, 	he
decided he would go to Milwaukee, Wisconsin.
There he went to work in the Linderman-Hoverson plant.

The physical requirements for these parts called for material
which would be more elastic with the physical strength in a higher
range. 	Elastic limit would be practically double that of gray iron
and it was not brittle in the same way that gray iron was.
My new job, 	of course, 	called for contacting these foundries.
Here again improving the patterns and mounting them on machines be-
gan to show the higher results that were obtained both in finish
and in the quality of the casting itself. 	These castings as they
came from the mold were very brittle. 	They were put through annealing
ovens in which temperature of about 2100° Fahrenheit was maintained.
In the very large furnaces the time requirement for putting the
casting through the process was very much longer than in the
smaller type of furnaces. 	This was evident because of the mass
that had to be brought up to temperature which came up very slowly
and then after it was held, 	it had to remain in the furnace for a
longer period in order to cool down to temperatures where it could
be removed. 	This, 	altogether, 	was a very expensive process. 	Later
on, when we got into alloy steel and made forgings, we were able
to again get better material at less cost, but in our period, 	it
was one of the necessary things to do in order to get materials
for the motor car.
Mr. Simpson was aggresive and very willing to improve his

In July of 1906 a momentous thing occurred that would

vanadium steel was poured at the United Steel Company in Canton,
Ohio.
For six months prior to this, 	we had had several visits
from Mr. 	J. Kent Smith. 	He was a noted English metallurgist.
Going back in the history of steel development, you will find

Steel Company of Canton, 	Ohio. 	After making his arrangements

greatest conservation properties that anyone can conceive. 	Necessarily

These water power plants were intended to broaden out the
manufacturing base. 	Already we could feel that concentrating too many
workers in one place was economically wrong. 	River Rouge plant was
the best evidence. 	We found workers driving fifty miles to work;
whereas, 	the community they lived in was crying for some industry. 	I
am convinced more than ever that industry must spread out its opera-
tions instead of condensing it.
I had to run into this problem again at Willow Run where
60,000 workers were needed in one plant. 	We could not keep a full
organization going. 	The turnover of labor was our real problem in
getting production on the B-24 bomber.
I 	started to break up the units into Highland Park, 	Lincoln
plant and Rouge plant; 	also some branches helped out and some inde-
pendent suppliers. 	When we brought these units into Willow Run, 	then
our 	labor requirements went down to 30,000. 	From then on the plant
came up to schedule of one plane per hour.
Evidence of decentralizing is to be seen around the Dettoit
area. 	The small close-in towns attract tool shops, 	engineering and
small parts makers. 	Large automobile makers are enlisted in de-
centralizing their operations. 	Henry Ford and I saw this as a neces-
sity in 1917.

The great mystery to the public in the early years of Ford
Motor Company was how it could grow into such an outstanding institu-
tion. 	It became the pattern for all mass production developnents.
There are factors that apply in the growth of all economics. 	This
period 1900-1910 was the advent of a new age. 	The prior 100 years,
1800-1900, was a period of disorganization. 	The country had to
survive one crisis after another.
The population vas growing rapidly. 	It was made up of
the overflow of J!nrope - 	capable, 	strong, 	and ambitious men and
women. 	It was the cream or these dissatisfied nations. 	They sub-
scribed to the pioneer life of this new country. 	They all came with
individual ideas. 	They felt like they were released from prison.
They spread all over the continent, and they were not hampered by-

All this natural reaction for transportation was neces-
sary to set up the demand and set the creators• minds to working
out the problem of producing automobiles in volume and at a price
that would induce buying~ 	It all lent confidence and absorbed the
energy of these energetic pioneers. 	With no restriction on their
time or classifying their skill it was a go-as-you-please with a
race for time and wide open competition.
Free thinkers were in evidence. 	It was like a chain
reaction, 	something like a California or Alaska gold rush. 	Every-
one was getting the feel of it. 	Speculation was there for the
gambler. 	The air was full of it. 	Organized finances and bankers
looked at it with apprehension. 	Like Ford Motor Company it was
backed by some local people with direct interest in the venture.
The record states that over 1,200 companies were organized
to produce automobiles. 	There were 2,000 different makes of cars.
The start was made in 1893 by Duryea brothers. 	By 1900 more motor
vehicles were sold than horse-drawn carriages and wagons. 	It was
estimated that there were 25,000,000 horses and 10,000,000 bicycles.
Did anyone believe that they were on their way out? 	Fifty years
later, 1950, the industry had produced 100,000,000 cars and trucks.
From 1950 on they had been producing an average of 5,000,000 cars
per year with no indication of flooding the markets.
IJ
225

Acc. 65 box 68-11 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Rouge Expansion"
The 1,200 original manufacturers has boiled down to ten
automobile manufacturers end five truck-makers. 	Ford Motor Company
started in 1903, ten years after Duryea. 	It started like most of
the 1,200 others did, with no real plans for the future. 	It had
a ear, the creation of Henry Ford to go on with. 	There was nothing
to scream about, and very limited funds to work with. 	Perhaps the
greatest asset was the determination of Henry Ford to build this
particular car he had been tampering with.
Whether it was good or bad or if it was going to be ac-
cepted by the pa.blic, no one had the least idea. 	If the structure
could hold together for a while, 	and I assure you that was upper-
most in all our minds, 	not one of the original group were sure of
what they had. 	Henry Ford was apprehensive. 	The lack of working
capital really scared him. 	I got that from him many times. 	His
determination to stick to his original design was the salvation of
the Company. 	Had they floundered around like the others, they would
have sunk with the rest of them.
Couzens and the board could not change Henry Ford's
determination. 	Couzens had to market what he had. 	He laid the
foundation of a great selling organization. 	It was the car that

One of the most interesting projects to me in the Ford
Motor Company was the development of our European branches. 	There
were two phases to this development. 	The first phase started with
the appointment of Perry as a Ford agent in England in 1905. 	He
had his office in London and sold the first cars shipped to England.
He set up other sub-dealers. 	Some of the first agents were Jimmy
Reese of Blake and Coe of Liverpool and R. 	L. 	Jobling of George &
Jobling of Newcastle-on-Tyne. 	That business is still being operated
today under his son, R. 	L. 	Jobling. 	A Mr. 	Richard Woodhead of
Woodhead's Garage, Southport, was a dealer up until 1919. 	George
Heath of Heath's Garage, Ltd., 	Birmingham ceased to be a Ford dealer
about 1921 but is still living in Birmingham. 	William Rootes was a
sub-dealer. 	He is the father of Sir Willima and Sir Reginald Rootes
who are the heads of the largest motor building company in England •
.t•lr. Rootes, Sr. 	is still alive today living inretirement. 	There
were others but the aboveIBmes were the prominent ones that I would
see when I visited with Perry in England.
A few other early dealers were: 	Percy Hendy of Percy
Hendy, 	Ltd., Vincent's Walk, 	Pound Tree Road, South Rampton. 	Another
agent that we all knew in Detroit was Henry Alexander. 	His father,
J. 	L. 	Alexander, was our agent in Edinburgh, Scotland. 	The
Alexanders were famous 	in England. 	They drove a Model T up Ben Nevis
which is the highest mountain in the United Kingdom. 	This stunt was

Acc. 65 box 67-5 Sorensen, Charles E.; "European Branches"
I was a director in all these companies. 	I 	started
swinging around these companies annually. 	There would be a board
meeting scheduled to fit my schedule. 	My records show that I made
twenty trips to Europe. 	Expansion of plans and policy were the
order of business at these meetings. 	On these trips I met n:any
prominent people in these different countries; 	kings, 	dukes,
industrialists.
King Christian of Denmark gave me a decoration, 	the
highest given to Danes who left Denmark and made good. 	It is called
the Ryder of Dannedro. 	(See photo under date of May 18, 1931.) 	I
received a decoration of the same kind from King Haakon of Norway.
This was presented to me by his ambassador to the United States,
Mr. Morgensterne. 	I also met Kaiser Wilhelm when he was living in
Doorn, Holland. 	I went there with his grandson, 	Prince Louis
Ferdinand. 	I lunched with him and his family. 	(See autographed
photograph.)
Dr. Heinrich Albert, 	chairman of our German company was
the first German ambassador to the United States after World War I.
He was one of the great lawyers in Germany. 	Through him, 	I met
Mr. Schacht, head of Germany's biggest bank. 	He was associated with
hitler. 	Mr. Schacht wanted me to go with him to meet Hitler. 	Dr.
Albert asked me not to ~o. 	He was not in sympathy with Hitler.
Schacht was one of the group that was tried by the Allied judges

The plant at Cologne was ideal for our production. 	It
was a miniature Rouge plant minus blast furnaces and steel mill.
I was there once a year. 	I stayed a few days before the board
meetings and discussed plans with the staff. 	We drew on Copenhagen
for a manager and I brought E. 	Vitger in as works manager and a
young mechanic with the same name as mine -- Sorensen.
Berlin had a sales branch on Unter Den Linden with no
staff for manufacturing. 	It did not seem wise to put in foreigners.
Men from the United States, 	if they were not Germans, would not fit
in. 	We decided the Danes could come in. 	They spoke German. 	We
wanted to make it all German so Vitger and Sorensen were delegated
to train the staff. 	Dr. 	Albert finally brou7ht in Dr. 	Jur Erich
Diestl for managing director in 1930.
I had a young German in South America named Heinie who
went there on the Ford.son tractor work. 	He had his experience
originally on tractors in Dearborn. 	He was now in Berlin on the
sales of tractors. 	I brought him to Cologne under Vitger in 1931.
Germany was soon under the Hitler Government and the cry was
"Germany only for Germans." 	Under Diestl we qualified under that.
Perry and I were the only outside directors. 	I offered my
resignation to Dr. 	Albert but none of the directors would even
consider it.
The nationalists under Hitler watched Cologne. 	They were

was laying his plans for war. 	Re wanted all the trucks and car
makers pulled back into Germany. 	They termed Cologne in the danger
zone. 	Dr. 	Albert was on the spot. 	He would not do this without
our consent. 	He came to Dearborn to see Henry Ford and Edsel. 	He
tried to get my support on complying with the Hitler program.
Albert wanted to affiliate Cologne and some ether motor
manufacturers in the central Germany area. 	He got nowhere in
Dearborn. 	His standing suffered with Henry Ford. 	It was a bad
shock for him. 	He flet the move was necessary to retain our position

but he was told we would risk what we had and if that failed, 	Hitler
would be in the motor car business.

On June 1, 1934 I was back in Paris. 	Perry, Mathis and
Dollfus were there. 	Sunday, 	June 3, 	we were at the Mathis home

the Mathis plant with our Ford Company. 	It was the idea of the
French directors for a national plant. 	It was clear that Perry
was not in accord with this. 	I was there as an observer with no
prejudices.
There was a running battle between Perry and Dollfus.
We discussed using Mathis and his plant. 	I wanted to see it before
I heard any more talk.

found Mathis had a small but well-equipped plant. 	Mathis was

Upon my return from England in August, 1917, 	I was pleased
and surprised to find Henry Ford waiting-for me when I arrived in
Detroit on the morning train. 	My wife and son were there. 	He
came along with us in my car. 	It was nice to be home again. 	I
had a strenuous three months in England with this war program and
with little chance to contact home or the offices. 	I was sure
happy to be back.
In the short visit I had with Henry Ford he told me that
I would meet a new man that F.dsel had sent to Dearborn. 	His name
was Ernie Kanzler. 	He told me that he was Edsel's brother-in-law.
I told him I would be around in the morning. 	I wanted this day
to myself and my family. 	I wanted to get acquainted again.
Henry Ford called me that evening on the phone. 	He was
Tery kind and anxious to get details of my trip. 	I was in the
plant early and had a reunion with all the staff. 	Kanzler was

the next morning Henry Ford picked me up at my home and we went
to Highland Park where we spent the day with Edsel.

This stationary electrical furnace held twenty tons. 	Al-
loy additions were made here. 	This furnace was charged from the
cupola melt. 	All our back stock, like pouring gates and rejected
castings, 	as well as iron borings in cans, were smelted in the
cupola and charged into the stationary electric furnace. 	We also
added pig iron to the cupola charge. 	This was made in our own
blast furnace and this was part of the Foundry layout.
This was a revolutionary method. 	It was developed within
the organization. 	There was nothing like it operating on a large
scale anywhere. 	To be able to make a continuous operation of this
took out all the back-breaking work from the molding and pouring
and handling of the casting as it crone out of the mold.
Cooling of the casting after pouring was important in
order to control hardness and cracking. 	These controls produced
a casting that went through the machine operations with added life
to the cutters in the milling machines, drills, 	and reruners. 	The
speed of all operations was stepped up. 	This, 	of course, 	showed
us the need of new machinery to meet this casting develo:pnent.
New cutting steels that could handle higher speeds added
to the new development. 	The V-8 casting by the nature of its design
was a solid, rigid block. 	It would now stand any load that machines
could put on it. 	This became a Pattern and Foundry development. 	On
the drafting board it could not be visualized. 	I enjoyed some of

type by our closest competitors.
The present-day 1954 V-8 engine has adapted overhead valves.

The announcement in April of 1935 that we were going to
erect additional open hearths and a Rolling Mill brought in applica-
tions for every operation in the mill. 	As the layout progressed
with the engineers of the United Engineering Company they contracted
to build the entire plant and supervise and employ all the super-
intendents. 	I hired them on our staff to follow up the erection and
inspect all work as it was being installed. 	The United Engineering
assumed full responsibility for workmanship and all the mills and
furnaces to come up to their rated capacity.
A description of this Steel Mill in simple language will
go like this: 	West of the Rouge slip and south of the main roadway
through the plant are two mammoth units devoted to the manuf'acturer
of steel, the Open Hearth and the Steel Mill. 	Every operation of
that manufacture from the Open Hearth furnaces to the Rolling Mill
is included there. 	The building at the south end houses the former
and is 238 feet wide by 1,066 feet long. 	Ten furnaces, 	each having
a normal capacity of 100 tons per heat 	(two a day) form the melting
equipnent.
391

Acc. 65 box 67-3 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Engineering Design and Philosophy"
The molten steel is poured through a Ford-developed
pouring dish having four outlets into four ingot molds standing
on a Ford-designed car. 	Each car holds eight molds and ·each ingot
weighs 1,700 pounds. 	The molds are hauled by train into the south
end of the Steel Mill, 	the soaking pit section. 	Here the ingots
are lifted out of Ford-designed prong cranes, capable of lifting
four at one time, 	and dropped into a conveyer which takes them past
the line of pits. 	There are nine stacks or furnaces, 	each divided
into four compartments. 	From two and one-half to three hours are
required to bring cold ingots to a rolling temperature, much less
when the ingots arrive at the pits at red heat.
The ingots after soaking are lifted from the pits by
crane and dropped into a chute which connects to a series of re-
volving rollers. 	The ingot run delivers the white hot ingots to the
Blooming Mill. 	This mill is capable or rolling more tonnage than
any other in the world.
After the ingot has passed through four stands of forty-two
inch rolls, 	it becomes a bloom. 	Four stands of thirty-two inch
rolls transforms the bloom into a billet. 	From a transfer table

The idea of a two-story press shop came from me. 	With
presses on the upper floor and all finished stampings dropping from
the presses to the lower floor, 	it permitted setting presses close
together with conveyers handling sheets as required. 	This plan
kept every operation moving through the presses with the lower

the Steel Mill ended up under cranes at the end of the Pressed

blanking presses cut up these coils and dropped all scrap down into
the lower floor. 	This two-story plan was the source of much discus-
sion. 	Again I repeat that architects were never involved in these
discussions.

Following these letters, Mr. 	Jeffers talked to me on the
telephone and told me that Mr. Roosevelt, 	President of the United
states, had told him to prepare facilities for making tires to be
shipped to Russia.
The President in his talks with our Russian allies on
their needs for tires for war vehicles had promised them he would
send the necessary equipment to make tires. 	He delegated Mr. 	Jeffers
to find the equipment. 	The President impressed the great importance
of this connnitment on Mr. Jeffers and he delegated Mr. 	Jeffers to
find the equipment. 	Jeffers wrote similar letters like the one I
quoted to other tire-makers.
Here was a complete plant available. 	He showed this to
the President, and he ordered Jeffers to pick it up and get it to
Russia at once.
We agreed on the price of the entire plant. 	We also made
a layout that showed how to transplant the equipnent to its new site.
Every machine had a number on its crate and a number plate on the
machine itself. 	A Russian crew stood by until it was all loaded
aboard ship in the Rouge plant docks.

In Highland Park Fdward Gray sold Henry Ford on building
a steam-gas engine power plant. 	He was hired to build a pair of
engines and build the powerhouse. 	The engines were built by Hooven,
Owens, Rentschler in Hamilton, 	Ohio. 	The chief engineer there
was William Mayo. 	He came into the Power plant and acted as a con-
sultant in the designing of the steam plant. 	These two men then
developed power plants for the branches we were building in the
United States. 	These two men would plan these installations
and select architects to draw the plans. 	They would be sent out
for bids and F.d Gray would follow them through.
Let me point out again that the original design was done
in Highland Park with Henry Ford passing on it. 	Architects who
got in on these new plants would take credit and advertise that
they created them. 	This was annoying to Gray and Henry fi'ord but it
was a practice used by advertising companies to satisfy architects.
It was a way they displayed their wares and it is still being
used.

A study of the fact could have shown that this original
assembly work was not done in 1913; 	it actually was done in 1908,
at which time Mr. Henry Ford did not start the thing by shouting
"Go" and holding a stop watch in his hand.
The advertisement also states that Henry Ford first got
the idea of how to assemble motor cars in a Chicago packing plant.
Henry Ford never did see a Chicago packing plant. 	A comparison
of a Chicago packing plant and our assembly line was used by some
writers for comparison. 	Certainly we were familiar with it, but it
never for a moment had a:ay bearing on our thinking so far as as-
sembling motor cars. 	I must completely contradict this story that
Mr. 	Ford did all these things.
Mr. Ford on the contrary was always telling the public
that it was an organization that he had that was doing this.
Certainly better and more honest publicity can be prepared than I
have seen in this article.
(Picture of assembly line operation)

The period from 1903 to 1913 was the Couzens control.
The period from 1913 to 1925 was the Henry Ford and

The period from 1925 to 1944 was the Sorensen control,

The historic board meeting held on November 2, 1916
settled the D18.tter of erecting a plant on the River Rouge site.
The record I have set out showing the stages of development qualified
the foresight of Henry Ford. 	In the f'e.ce of the opposition of
his directors his final plan of' the River Rouge plant won them over,
all except the Dodge brothers. 	They still had a finger in the cash
box. 	They took some hard consistent work to get them free of it.
The key to the Rouge project was deep water into the
property. 	In August, 1917 an appropriation bill for dredging the
Rouge was passed by the United States Senate. 	On August 14, 1917
the directors of the Ford M:>tor Company in a fbrmal resolution
instructed the officers to go ahead on deepening the River Rouge and
to dredge out a turning basin on the property of the Company.
Going be.ck to the Mayo letter, it merely confirmed what
we had decided before Mr. Ford began acquiring the property in 1915.
We had already covered the water front in a motorboat with which we
traveled the length of the River Rouge and all the water front on
Detroit River. 	There we studied every possible piece of land available.
Mayo's letter was a confirmation of all our studies of' the area. 	The
planning of the Rouge plant was controlled in the Dearborn plant of'
Henry Ford & Son. 	I set up a separate room with large tables with the
property laid out to scale.

I 	suspect the Rouge plant was as well a considered plan as it was

a shot in the dark. 	Mr. Ford's determination to put a plant on that
spot was sufficient to 	start the cycle.
In the usual way that I had followed since I tirst met
him, 	I started on the layout boards in Dearborn to lay out in model
form what each building would look like. 	The ship canal was now
located with docks or dock and blast furnaces all built up on one-
eighth of an inch to the foot scale with roads, 	railroads, 	highways,
bus lines, and streetcars all fitted in to serve the immediate and
future plans. 	It was the only way that Henry Ford could see the
ultimate plan.
The Rouge Foundry by- its very nature had to be a super
plant. 	The need for such a plant was amplified by' our experience
in Highland Park. 	Even Highland Park was the best we knew how to
do based on prior experience; at first local foundries, 	then our
own first venture in a foundry which we acquired in Romeo, Michigan,
and later the foundry at Highland Park, which was a step in advance.
The Foundry superintendent was James Miller. 	I brought
him along when I was promoted to assistant superintendent in Piquette.
I first met Miller when he was an apprentice in Bryant and Berry.
The question of a foundry for the Rouge was studied in
the office of Miller et Highland Park Foundry. 	I took Harry Hanson

The history of the Rouge plant parallels the history of
the automobile, which represents a large slice of the history of
American progress in the twentieth century. 	It reflects our in-
dustrial development, our technical achievements, and our restless
drive toward better living for all of us. 	Now I believe this will
continue. 	It just can't be stopped, for the development of the
-
automobile is far from being finished. 	The strides we have made
are great but there are many more just ahead. 	Look at the current
advance in engines, 	in both automobiles and airplanes. 	The studies
being made on fuel are a great achievement in itself.
We did not know anything about fuel in 1903. 	We only
knew an engine could be ma.de to run on it; that was all. 	l-ruch funda-
mental pioneering work must be done to make a theory for an idea
shaped up in iron or steel. 	It often means a break with tradition
or other accepted practices. 	It calls for positive action and the
ability to plan and organize. 	It takes men with talent and vision

to reach new goals or standards, but never being able to reach the
ultimate is the best thing for all of us. 	To believe the moment
a thing is created that it is out of date is sound thinking.
Up to this point I have tried to show how we operated within
the Ford Motor Company on a major project. 	The Rouge plant was
studied and considered in Dearborn. 	It started with Henry Ford
promoting the idea. 	The over-all planning was done by Sorensen and
Henry Ford. 	The engineering was finalized by Mayo and Hanson.
There was no budgeting or appropriating of funds. 	The board passed
a plan knowing only what the Blast Furnace and Coke Oven plants would
cost. 	Fdsel was the treasurer and accepted profit knowing that more
was to come. 	I kept him advised on the over-all plan and he was in
full accord. 	We never prepared estimates on the immediate or over-all
cost or the project. 	There was the understanding that the plant was
needed as soon as possible. 	To move forward full speed was our aim.

the outside stockholdings were purchased by the Ford family, 	Fdsel

The individual cases that Henry Ford would take interest
in would be those that came to him through the Ford Motor Company's
Sociological Department or now and then a cijse that would be heard
about from the newspapers.
Back in the country near his farms he would find families
in need. 	Sometimes there would be a widow with children or a family
where the husband was ill. 	When he found such mses he would lend
help directly. 	If there was a boy or girl old enough to go to
work, he would arrange so they could earn as much in the plant as
the head of the family could. 	Medical aid would be added if necessary.
While he was helping out he would observe how they were
reacting to his help. 	He expected them to improve their ways of
living. 	He did not call this charity. 	He did not like the word
charit y . 	He felt it was abused when not properly followed up.
In the Sociological Department where he tried to lend aid,
he found resentment from many of the cases. 	There was too much
follow-up and checking. 	Henry Ford felt the department was too
bureaucratic. 	He was never satisfied that charity could be dealt
out on a methodical plan. 	He tried in his own way.
One day on his way to the Highland Park plant, 	he saw a
man walking along the road. 	He looked old and worn, a typical tramp.
Henry Ford stopped his car and talked with him. 	He asked him where
he was going. 	He learned he was bound for Detroit. 	Did he have a

he was struggling so, 	but he pushed himself through a world which he
did not comprehend until, 	by accident, 	he came in contact with Henry
Ford.
Since then, he has had no trouble in finding his way. 	To
find Ford'e way became his goal, 	and he has applied almost superhuman
energy to the task. 	To-day he looks like a half-back at the height
of the season. 	If he has ever been tired, no one has heard about it.
And if anyone in his domain reaches a point where he is more interested
in other things than in work Sorensen is all for giving him a permanent
vacation.
Work is Sorensen's religion. 	He, 	too, 	is not clubby. 	If a
man doesn't seem to making good, Sorensen is strangely patient with him
and will go to no end of trouble to find out why. 	Paradoxically, 	it is
the man who does seem to be making good toward whom he seems impatient.
Sorensen has the reputation of driving such people pretty hard.
There's a reason. 	I don't pretend to have solved the riddle
of Sorensen in a few hours of acquaintance, but I 	caught on to one of
. his basic principles. 	Modern industry has no stopping-place. 	An
individual may:te.ve a stopping-place, but industry must go on faster
and fa~ter. 	Since industry is driven by men, however, 	it is up to the
executive to release all the power he can find in everybody. 	If a man's
ambition can not be whetter, 	it isn't necessary to throw him on the
scrap-heap. 	He can hold A job in the ranks, where all he will have to
do is to keep up with the crowd -- and in an industry as large as Ford's
one can find a place which even cripples and otherwise handicapped
persons can fill. 	But if a man does develop ambition, 	it is up to the
executive to ride this ambition to its utmost possible limit.

Our relations with the Russians continued right up until
World War II started in 1941. 	Amtorg was the New York office that
handled all our matters such as placing orders and making payments.
Shipping regulations and material control was taken over by the
United States Government. 	This put an end to our direct contact
with the .Amtorg Corporation. 	Direct contact was completely cut off.
I did not hear from any or my Russian friends. 	This contact took
place after I had left the Ford Motor Company.
I resigned March 1, 1944. 	I was at my home at Miami Beach,
Florida on that date. 	On May 15, 	I was back in Detroit. 	I will
quote for a note I have in my diary under date or May 15, 1944.
"Donald Nelson phoned me at Farmington. 	Merrill Meigs also phoned
me all about Russia."

Prior to this, 	I had had a few hints from some of my
Washington friends 	that President Roosevelt had been considering
sending a commission to Russia; 	that he wanted me to head this
commission. 	The telephone call that I have referred to on May 15
from Donald Nelson confirmed what I had previously heard. 	Nelson
stated that the President had spoken to him about this co11111.ission
and for me to head it up.
A few days later when I went to Washington to discuss the
matter with him, 	Nelson told me about a trip that he had just made
to Russia himself. 	While he was in Moscow, he learned how I had
met up with many of the officials there. 	He met many of the men
that I had been dealing with in Russia, 	of whom I had made ms.ny
staunch friends. 	Nelson got an over-all picture of what had been
accomplished and he was so impressed with it that when he came back
to Washington, he reported this to President Roosevelt.
Roosevelt was impressed with Nelson's trip. 	He felt it
was building good relationship with Russia and he wanted that contact
continued. 	He asked me to come to Washington and discuss the matter.
Nelson also stated that Mr. Averill Harriman, who was then
the ambassador to Mosco~ was in Washington. 	He asked me as soon as
I 	arrived in Washington would I please call on Mr. Harriman and
discuss the matter with him. 	He particularly asked me to get there
as soon as I possibly could.

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
Late in 1907 Henry Ford began talking about making a tractor
for farming purposes. 	The first one was made at Piquette out of parts
from the Model B. 	It was taken out to the farm for testing by Bert
Scott. 	Then Henry Ford wanted to build a better tractor. 	He did not
want this to conflict with what we were doing at the Piquette Avenue
plant.
Early one morning in 1909 I went with him over to a resi-
dence on Woodward Avenue just about three blocks from the plant, where
there was a big barn in the rear that had been used for horses and
carriages with a large loft overhead. 	We decided that this was a
suitable place for putting in a few draftsmen and developing a new
unit. 	We rented it right there on the spot, 	gave the place a thorough
house cleaning and then we moved in a little organization with Joe
Galamb to head it up.
In this tractor we used the copper water-jacketed, 	four-
cylinder engine that we had taken out o.f the Model T.
After it was completed we sent it out to Mr. Ford's farm
where he put it tow:>rk first mowing hay, 	then harvesting oats and
wheat and he adapted the tractor to the tools that he had on the farm.
All together during the summer we built three of these
vehicles and kept them in service throughout the fall season with
plowing, and during the winter we did a lot of manure spreading. 	Of
course, 	in manufacturing this tractor, 	it did not come up obviously

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
This would tell us the size of the engine required and gear ratios in
the final drive. 	The engine would be a cast gray iron block, 	crank
case would be the same. 	The shifting gear transmission was settled
on so we added on a cast iron gear housing which, when it was assembled
to the engine, made a wonderful strong unit and the oil circulation in
the transmission and engine was the same.
The final drive on most of these other tractors was either
by chain or'some outside form of drive which was in the dirt that was
being thrown up in farming operations. 	Our decision was that we would
put this drive inside of a housing just like it was on a motor car.
We then decided that we would make a worm and a worm wheel
drive to the rear wheels. 	'While this did not look like the most ef-
ficient type of a drive, 	it was evident that we could provide plenty
of safety and with a little additional horsepower we would have a
thoroughly closed in, 	sealed up operation all around with no wearing
parts on the outside.
The final drive became a cast iron housing in which the
worm and worm wheel were bronze castings attached to the end of the
transmission housing for the axle shafting. 	A belt housing was also
made of cast iron. 	The' axle shafts were fitted to the axle housing.
!he inner end of the shaft was splined so that it would enter the
differential housing when bolting the axle housing to the center
housing.
642

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
With this important detail settled there was then the matter
of creating an axle and a drive and an engine for the drive and a
front end for controlling the direction.
We then decided on what we termed the three unit system.
From the rear end of the engine we designed a transmission that went
into a housing which also contained the worm and the worm wheel and
the differential housing. 	The two outside shafts to the wheel drive
were then enclosed in the housing so that made what we termed the
rear unit. 	The outer housings were all cast iron.
The engine with a flywheel and a clutch and a front end
cover, to which we could fasten the axle and steering device, was
the center unit. 	These were so designed that we could throw three
units on a set of rails which would be used for bringing the units
together on and then put them ahead on to a conveyor. 	It was all
designed with the Ford idea in mind of making a unit of it on a
conveyor. 	We prepared the design in ninety days. 	I then put orders
for fifty tractors into the shop.
We started out by putting in a pattern shop and then a
little foundry was built. 	We set this off a little way back from
the building on the front of the street. 	I set it back far enough
so we would have some room between it and the main building where
we could put in some machinery for making up the units. 	I was
able to buy the worm drive, 	composed of a wheel and a worm, 	from

In the tractor plant I 	could do anything I wanted to.
Henry Ford would not bother with details.
Throughout the season of 1916, from spring until late fall,
we kept our tractors in continued operation. 	They did all the
farming work on Mr. 	Ford's farm. 	There was a good deal to learn on
the tractors themselves. 	The design however was good. 	From the

before the season was over. 	The work we were carrying on was
receiving a great deal of publicity from newspapers and magazines.
We were soon having visitors from all over the world in to have a
look at what we were doing.
I particularly want to mention that Lord Northcliff of
England came to see us. 	He spent several days on the tractor,
driving it himself, 	and looking at the ones that we had torn down
for examination. 	He was much impressed with it and the effect of
his thinking was carried back to England.
This work was a'.11 going on during the war period, 	and in
England food was one of the 	serious problems. 	Northcliff went
away convinced that England should as soon as possible get into
farm tractor production for use on the estates and the farms in
England. 	There was a serious food problem in England. 	He left
with the idea that he could do a great deal to alleviate the farming
problem by getting on with the tractor program.

647

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
that he would immediately dispatch Sorensen with blueprints and
patterns, 	that he would lend every aid possible to getting this
tractor into production in England. 	We felt that we had had
enough experience with it to know that it would meet all the
requirements for farming purposes.
We also asked Perry to arrange for priority for all
the equipment that we wanted to bring over as well as for trans-
portation for myself as well as the staff that I would bring
with me. 	All these requests were cleared immediately.
I filled an old express car full of tractor parts as
well as patterns and farming implements to go with it. 	On
Saturday, April 25 everything was ready to leave and we took a
train for Halifax, Nova Scotia. 	Henry Ford as well as Gordon
McGregor, 	of our Canadian branch, went along with me. 	I also
had in our party Mead Bricker, John Crawford, 	Brady Burghoff,
Arthur Dugrey and William Jackson. 	These men were all members
of our Dearborn staff.
We arrived in Halifax, Nova Scotia on Saturday morning,
April 28, after a forty-eight hour trip from Detroit. 	We were
met by officials here who knew of our mission, who were prepared
to give us every help and attention necessary.
Saturday night we went to dinner with Mr. Ford and Mr.
McGregor to meet the Premier of the province, Mr. 	Honorable G. 	H.

We could see shells dropping into the big cathedral which stood up

We got down to Paris in the afternoon. 	We went immediately
to Citroen's plant where he had a very interesting layout. 	We had
put up a new building for this plant. 	There was no steel or any
ordinary construction materials available at the time. 	He had
brought in tree trunks about ten inches in diameter which he used
for posts for supporting his roof. 	The whole building was con-
structed of wood and 	cut green. 	It had no proper windows in it
but it was well lighted. 	He had quite a battery of Potter &
Johnson automatic lathes which he put his bar stock in and turned
out his shells.
I spent the afternoon and very late into the night
covering these operations with him and talking with his production
manager. 	They listed needs of a lot of machinery that should go
into the plant. 	I took this list of equipment and told him I

During this period of development Ferguson could not
see the program of the work. 	It was going much too fast for
him to follow the details. 	I tried to get him in often and
regularly. 	I was pleased to get his suggestions. 	I have
always had one trouble in my life. 	I had more ideas than I
could keep up with. 	Mr. 	Ford piled on his ideas. 	Ideas piled
in when new ideas are being cleaned up for production.
Mr. 	Edsel Ford was interested, 	and I added his ideas
to it. 	All the members of my staff were brought in to clear
up the details. 	I must say that Ferguson bothered me and my
staff less than anybody.
In this period Mr. Ford was inspecting his ideas of
a worm drive for the tractor. 	Sheldrick and I were sceptical
on this, and it never did come through. 	Was there any Ferguson
control there? 	No. 	He was in the 	same position I was with Mr.
Ford, 	but I did not hold back on the final beveled gear drive
in place of a final worm drive. 	I worked this along with the
other one.
Sheldrick did the engineering and we were all ready
when the worm drive was rejected by Mr. Ford. 	No one could
maneuver around this planning like I 	could. 	He was not able
to do it, nor did he know how. 	He was most careful to see that
he remained in Mr~ 	Ford's good graces. 	He was happy to see
721

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
Charlie do it, and he learned how to respect me all the more
for it, because much time was gained on the final 	job.
The plow that Ferguson brought over and which he
says that he perfected was not acceptable to Mr. 	Ford. 	It
was a handmade implement from materials at hand, 	such as bars
of steel, plate and angles. 	It was a blacksmith's job. 	It
was what Ferguson had to work with.
The implement was not designed for volume production.
While the implement could give a demonstration for a short time
like it did for Mr. 	Ford, 	it was evident on close study by Mr.
Ford and myself that it would not give the service required
by the farms 	in this country. 	This 	judgement we used we could
qualify by comparing the details of American-made plows used
on the rough, 	stony, 	heavy soils in this country.
I can illustrate. 	The beam he used ~as a bar of
steel, 	wider than it was thick. 	This beam had to be heated
and formed. 	It was shaped like a capital letter J. 	The mold
boards and chairs were mounted on the bent end. 	The other
end was attached to the tractor. 	This bar had holes drilled
through it at different places to put bolts through in the
assembly. 	When holes are drilled through a bar in the section
where they are drilled, 	a weakness occurs. 	Therefore, 	this
plow had a series of weak spots. 	There was too much metal in
722

Acc. 65 box 68-15 Sorensen, Charles E.; "Tractors"
some sections and not enough in others. 	A flat bar is weak
one way and stronger the opposite way.
The direction where the strength was needed most was
the weakest section. 	Without waiting to consult Ferguson, who
was in Ireland now, 	and not wanting any further delay, Mr.
Ford and I proceeded to develop a plow with the proper design
for production and assembly.
Ferguson knew nothing about the physical strength of
the materials used and even less about the heat treat require-
ments. 	I proposed that the beam itself, which was the back-
bone of the plow, be designed so it could be cast of a suitable
alloy steel of high physical value. 	In casting form we could
make an H section wider where the greatest strain came and
sufficient material around the holes so they were not the weak
spots. 	This design was made by our own engineers, 	assisted by
Mccarroll, 	the metallurgist and our foundryman.
We designed some testing fixtures to test the beam
under all loads in every direction. 	We came up with a beam
that weighed no more and was double the strength compared with
the bar beams. 	All holes were cast in place. 	It was cast
true to size and shape, 	and it required very little machine
work. 	The ability to make them all a like in shape and size
did away with a lot of heat treating, 	bending the bar to fit
gauges.

(July 5: 	lunch at Dearborn. 	Henry Ford and F.dsel there.
Talked more about aviation engines. 	Will go ahead on this at once.)
We decided to design a V-12-cylinder engine, 	size and
horsepower equivalent to the Rolls-Royce. 	I had Sheldrick prepare
a general specification. 	We delegated the design work to Van Ranst.
We decided to do some research on superchargers.
Mechanical-driven blowers were used on the Rolls-Royce. 	A train
of gears would drive a blower up to 12,000 rpm. 	We decided to use
an exhaust-driven blower; 	this would put some back pressure on
the exhaust.
We would also do some research on fuel injection. 	The
possibilities seemed to be there. 	I will elaborate on this engine
after we did some research.
On July thirty~first Henry Ford was seventy-seven years
old. 	While he agreed to our developing an aviation engine, there
was no hint of it being used in any war program. 	On his birthday,
while the reporters were after him for a birthday interview, 	one
or them asked him if he believed we were going to war. 	He refused
to talk about that.
Arter they left he was .furious. 	It was easy to stir up
his emotions on a discussion about war. 	He told me that the United
states was not going to get into war. 	He could hardly say anything
else; he was a confirmed pacifist. 	Op his birthday he was not

He had studied the vulnerability of' these engines for
air battle. 	Liquid-cooled engines would have much larger profiles.
The engine length, the cooling pipes, radiator, and pumps increased
the profile, 	and any missile or flack striking these parts would
cripple the engine.
In World War I the United states, 	England, 	and Germany
used liquid-cooled engines. 	France had an air-cooled engine that
made a reputation for itself'.
I vas interested in Lindbergh's explanation. 	It has
always remained in my mind how right he was. 	All United states
Air Force planes had air-cooled engines in World War II.

(April 21: 	Arrived Detroit 8 :15. 	Met Henry Ford in

(April 22: 	Henry Ford II now ensign in the Navy, 	stationed

I was disappointed to see the young man take the step.
I hoped that he would tie in with our airplane program. 	I wanted
to sign his application for exemption, but he would not do that.
He said that there was so much fuss made when his father was exempted
that he did not want that to happen to him.
He took a definite stand on it and he enlisted. 	The
plant had gone back to work on April twenty-third. 	There were
79,000 men in that day, 91,000 before the strike.
( April 24: 	To the Rouge plant. 	Talked to General Wolf
when he brought in a B-24 bomber to Dearborn. 	Henry and F.dsel
had a good look at it. 	It gave us a wonderful performance.)
I had been asking Wolf to do this so I could get Henry
Ford to see what we were going to build. 	He saw a fine demonstration
but he was only mildly interested. 	Nothing for him to be enthused
over. 	It was a war plane.
The unions have been very annoying to him these past weeks.
He usually sees Bennett the first thing in the morning. 	That starts
him off in a bad humor. 	Can't get him out of it.
I hoped the new plane and air engine would give him some

On May 15, 	1941, Wibel and Roscoe &nith were in Washington

I have tried to show how we tackled the B-24 bomber.
I have covered about ninety days of contacting by Government and
ourselves. 	I have filled in only the high spots in this report.
I don't want to have it look like a mysterious, difficult job. 	I
particularly wanted to show the ones who were involved -- not many,
as you can see. 	It is one good reason why we could work fast.

He was unfair, 	but he gloried in keeping up the row between
father and son.
F.dsel was not well. 	It scared me to see the agony he
was in at times. 	On one of our trips ·to Washington we,had a sea.
food dinner in a famous restaurant. 	He took sick. 	I think the
oysters upset himo 	We had adjoining rooms. 	I got a doctor in
when we got to our room. 	He was in agony all night. 	I 	sat up
with him all night. 	I was afraid it was his end. 	He was to
have a long siege with stomach ulcers.
Strain and stress is the worst thing for that ailment.
I never saw Henry Ford have any sympathy for him when he was ill.
He kept preaching that he must learn to eat and live right. 	I
saw enough of that to make me feel bitter against Henry Ford. 	If
Eisel could have dropped out of sight for a while at this time
and gone in for a cure, he would have recovered from those ulcers.
Tn the many talks I had with him, 	he realized his condi-
tion. 	He promised that he was going to be less active and consider
his health. 	The doctors strongly advised him to get away from
all his worries. 	But actually, he never followed this advice.
The sons leaving their work and joining the Navy and Air Force was
a blow to him. 	He wanted them near him. 	We talked about that
every time we had a few moments together. 	Many times 	I left him
with a tear in my eye. 	Much like his father, 	when he was determined

was determined to do something, nothing could change him. 	I would

Nineteen forty-one was a year for preparing for the war
projects. 	It meant the end of the motor car business. 	There
would be some cars,and trucks for the Army, but our normal business
was at a standstill. 	Gasoline would be distributed on priority.
Agents would have their service and parts on a priority, 	an~ licenses
would be issued by the Government for all essential materials.
Actually, Ford Motor Company was now a Government sub-
sidiary. 	I was sitting in a meeting discussing plans with Wilson
of General Motors, 	Keller of Chrysler, 	and the other motor car
manufacturers. 	Army and Air Force officers were in the plant
constantly. 	It was bound to be a complex business under high
pressure. 	Everything was wanted yesterday.
On the bomber the most important thing to me was the
material required. 	There was no sense in building a plant if
there was no material.
On my trip to San Diego in June, 	I checked up with Fleet
on aluminum sheets. 	I learned that he was having shortages. 	He
was disturbed with the added requirements for our program. 	He
adTised me to go into this with Knudsen. 	He said he had had several
talks with him about the capacity of the aluminum milss. 	He warned
me that if this was not stepped up, 	there would be no more planes.

I wondered now if we were going in for this war program
a little too strong. 	Certainly the Government wanted us in to
the limit. 	I will have to pull the stops now. 	Organization is
what determined that. 	Normally, 	one could pull in more staff, but not
now. 	This type of work calls for experienced engineers and mechanics.
The supply has dried up. 	From now on we must build our own staff.
Nineteen forty-one, 	I repeat, 	is the year for rebuilding.
All the economies must be upset. 	A complete readjustment of our
facilities, 	as well as 	all others, 	is a new experience for all of
us.
The 1914 to 1918 war was nothing compared with this. 	One
can 1t 	say no to the demands of Washington if you have any capacity
left. 	The material demands were complicated by priorities. 	To
clean this up looked like a bigger problem than the production
itself.
I was working night and day. 	Meals and sleep became
irregular. 	One does not take that seriously because one believes
that he will get on top of the job and in that way get relief.
It got me down. 	First with me is my job.

(October 22: 	Fdsel and I had a hot session with Harry
Bennett. 	He blew up proper.)
Bennett had been telling Henry Ford about how Henry II
and Benson were in danger of being kidnaped. 	He wanted to
surround them and Fdsel's house with his servicemen. 	F.dsel
told Bennett to stop his talk and let the boys alone. 	He did
not want him protecting them or him. 	He told him he was satisfied
that Bennett had played up to Henry Ford and he did not believe
it.
Bennett put on his act. 	He threatened F.dsel, got up
from his chair, tore off his coat. 	I got up and motioned for
F.dsel to leave, which he did. 	:Efl.sel walked out ahead of me. 	It
is difficult to imagine such a scene but it will illustrate what
F.dsel was up against.
Henry Ford could have put an end to this but instead,
he encouraged Bennett to keep going. 	Constant turmoil was Henry
Ford ' s idea of harmony. 	He would keep things stirred up; then no
one could swell up with importance.
I had reached the stage where I was immune to that. 	Henry
Ford knew I could keep ahead of him in the plant. 	My standing in

A Government program, 	like Willow Run, 	could sponsor a
road from Detroit to Willow Run. 	A superhighway from the Rouge
plant to Willow Run could be the major road from Detroit to Chicago.
It would help our labor program getting in and out from east and
west. 	It would tap an area for twenty-five miles to give us
needed workers. 	We planned on 60,000, 	but with all our market
to draw on, we never got in over 42,000.
This meant we had to arrange our schedules putting units
into Lincoln, 	Highland Park, 	and Rouge. 	A grant from the Government
and the state got the superhighway going. 	It was finally a blessing
for the labor requirement. 	We developed the road plan with Kennedy.
He did a great job for Willow Run.

at $25,000 each. 	February 17, 1942, we received a further order

a grand total of 323 engines for the year. 	In March of 1942, when

The following March, 	in 1944, the production had now reached 1,894
engines per month, for a grand total of 25,789 engines.
The highest production was reached in March of 1945,
when the monthly production was 2,010 engines for the month.
This made a grand total of 51,052 engines. 	Total orders
amounted to more than 60,000 engines of which 57,851 were built
up to the seventeenth of August, 1945, when aircraft engine 	pro-
duction was ended. 	This March production was a grand total of
over 4,000,000 horsepower per month.

(June 4: 	First tank off the line.)
We assembled them in Highland Park under the high crane-
way buildings, perfect for the work. 	We assembled 1,690 tanks
in the full program.
(June 5: 	To Willow Run with Hanson on a barracks site.
Henry Ford came in to lunch at Dearborn. 	Three-thirty, 	Henry Ford
and I looked at our tank. 	It is a great outfit. 	He told me what
a problem Fdsel is to him. 	He is not satisfied about Bennett.)
The problem with F.dsel was that he wanted Fdsel to be
just like him. 	I know he loved him like a fat her would an only
son, which naturally meant that he expected great things from him.
Mrs. Ford was always in full accord with Henry Ford, 	so
like loving parents do, they wanted to completely tuck their child
under their wings and guide their child as they saw it. 	They could
not understand how Fdsel, being their son, 	could wander away on
his own.
Today in his talk with me 	I told him that trying to drive
Fdsel in line by using Bennett to annoy him and check his every
move was breaking down Edsel's respect for him. 	Fdsel could never
be a Henry Ford. 	He was seeking his own independence. 	Parent love
can go to extremes.
It was difficult for the folks to understand that F.dsel was
sick. 	The reason behind it? 	Fdsel, the son of a great man, tried
to be modest and unassuming. 	He never took advantage of his position.

$30,000 locked up on that job. 	I was being hounded by our auditors
for spending money which they felt we could not collect.
Eisel worried some about that, 	but he never blocked me.
I 	always kept him posted on these forward steps. 	He agreed with
me that we could justify what we had done.
I burned up the auditors to keep after General Quinten.
The general picked up some gray hairs on his job. 	He would sail
into me, but always ended up by meeting the bills. 	He was a
fine, 	able officer. 	I hope he has forgiven me for the worries
I gave him; 	I 1m sure he has.

(October 14: 	Gave Edsel the plan on deferring men
eligible for the draft. 	Lunch with Henry Ford.)
Manpower was the real problem now. 	Even, before, 	we were
losing men that we had on our production lines before we reached
full schedules. 	What was going to happen as the demands for more planes
came in? 	My plan was to defer any man from the draft if he stayed
on this work. 	We would set up a man-requirement schedule ahead.
If the draft boards could adapt themselves to that, then the labor
problem was simple. 	We had already learned that more women could be
used. 	We would draw on that to full Qllpaci ty. _
What possible. use could there be of a trained Army i.f
we could not produce the implements to go with it. 	Of course,
the draft board would not accept anything like that. 	I .felt that
if Knudsen and his people in Washington could make sense out of
this, 	they would balance the draft. 	Well, we tried all that.
Knudsen spent several days with me at Willow Run, trying
to solve this manpower problem. 	The tooling of Willow Run paid

producing complete bombers.

Henning had some pilots that he wanted to interview in Miami.

go with him.

Air Force. 	I was keen on making the trial run and studying its
perfonaance. 	We got a good weather report. 	Our flight plan was
filed; 	that meant that over the entire route we had a clearance
and could drop into any airport, and any air patrol would clear
us.

and I took the brunt of battling the complicated system that

Peace of mind is one of the essentials of health and
happiness. 	I feel that I am not contributing to your peace of
mind., 	as evidenced by your evading me and giving me your so-called
"silent treatment.n 	It is relected everywhere in your business.
I 	believe for my own peace or mind that I 	should take six months
ve,cation. 	Perhaps the abnormal situation we are both living in
contributes to this situation. 	Neither one of us has made too
many serious mistakes to reach our present situation, 	(whatever
that may be). 	But I would be happier if I 	felt 	I was contributing
to your peace of mind. 	I 	had many things I 	wanted to talk to you
about on my return from my visit in the South, but I feel that is
impossible for the moment; perhaps at some later time. 	I should
appreciate it if you would grant me six months leave. 	The war
program is in good hands in the plant., all up to full schedule.
My hope is six months from now it will be over. 	Maybe we can go
at it again and get back to normal?
Another note attached:
Most respectfully,
(unsigned)
Please accept my resignation as vice-president of Ford
Motor Company. 	I find it impossible to carry on the duties. 	If
it is agreeable to you I should like to take six months vacation.
My health requires a change. 	Last winter's virus pneumonia has taken
the steam out of me. 	I must change to a less severe climate. 	rr
· you will permit me this vacation that I need so badly after thirty-
eight years of continued application to my work, 	it will be a re-
ward for the many kindly things you have done for me.
